what: Statistical Tools for Medical Imaging NetCDF (MINC) Files
title: Statistical Tools for Medical Imaging NetCDF (MINC) Files
text: Tools reading, writing, analyzing, visualizing MINC neuroimaging files R. Supports voxel-wise linear models, mixed-effects models, correlations, ANOVA, FDR correction, TFCE, ROI-based anatomy analysis, surface-based vertex statistics, parallel execution. Unix (Linux, macOS). Requires R >= 4.0. native Windows support (WSL may work — see INSTALL).

title: Statistical Tools for Medical Imaging NetCDF (MINC) Files
text: RMINC depends MINC C library. two options: minc-toolkit-v2 (recommended): Download binary installers http://bic-mni.github.io/ build source https://github.com/BIC-MNI/minc-toolkit-v2. provides MINC library plus command-line tools. libminc source: toolkit found, RMINC’s configure attempt build libminc . requires CMake (> 2.6), git, HDF5 development headers. Debian / Ubuntu: macOS (Homebrew): macOS note: RMINC’s compiled backend needs Fortran compiler. Apple’s default clang include one — install gcc via Homebrew (brew install gcc libssh2 libgit2 libomp zlib) gfortran directly, point R via ~/.R/Makevars. See INSTALL file exact FLIBS/LDFLAGS setup detailed instructions.
code: sudo apt-get install libhdf5-dev cmake git lsof autoconf automake libtool brew install hdf5 cmake autoconf automake libtool

title: Statistical Tools for Medical Imaging NetCDF (MINC) Files
text: Fit voxel-wise linear model across set MINC volumes. response column data holds MINC file paths; model fit independently every voxel: Function-level documentation available R via ?mincLm, ?mincLmer, ?vertexLm, ?anatLm, etc.
code: library(RMINC)  # `gf` is a data frame whose `volumes` column holds MINC file paths fit <- mincLm(volumes ~ group, data = gf)  # Output columns: "F-statistic", "R-squared", beta coefficients, # t-statistics, "logLik". Write the whole-model F-statistic (column 1): mincWriteVolume(fit, output.filename = "group_Fstat.mnc", column = 1)

title: Anatomy Linear Mixed Effects Model — anatLmer
text: anatLmer, like relative mincLmer provides interface running linear mixed effects models every vertex. Unlike standard linear models testing hypotheses linear mixed effects models difficult, since denominator degrees freedom difficult  determine. RMINC provides estimating degrees freedom using anatLmerEstimateDF function. likely models - longitudinal models separate intercept separate intercept slope per subject - approximation likely correct. careful using approximations using complicated random effects structures.

title: Read Civet-Generated Dat Files — civet.readCivetDatFiles
text: list returned containing following components: native_tissue_volumes volumes, cubic millimeters, 3 primary classification components. Note values returned reflect volumes within native brain.  values computed dividing stereotactic volume values re-scaling factor comprised xScale * yScale * zScale (defined linear xfm file). gyrification_index gyrification index computed per hemisphere reflects degree gyrification cortical surface.  Value computed dividing cortical gray matter area area convex (smooth) hull area.  computation always yield number greater 1, larger numbers indicating greater gyrification. native_cerebral_volumes 3 values returned, one particular use.  value labeled “cortical_gray” reflects volume cortical gray matter native space brain.  “extra_cerebral_csf” value reflects volume extra-cerebral csf (.e., cortical surface within sulci), “wmSurface_plus_contents” value reflects volume white matter surface volume components encapsulated surface. volume measurements relative native space brain. quality_control values interest Civet developers.  values labelled “classify_qc” reflect proportion various components identified tissue classification. values percentages, applicable native stereotactic space.

text: Fit linear model every row response matrix, allowing one predictors vary row--row lock-step response. formula transform voxel-varying covariate (splines, squares, interactions, etc.), flexLm detects uses fast inner-product update model matrix instead re-evaluating formula every row. Otherwise falls back rebuilding model matrix per row.

title: Flexible Linear Model with Voxel-Varying Covariates — flexLm
text: formula linear model formula. left-hand side must literal name y; per-row response vector bound name internally. data data frame containing model terms constant across rows y. one row per observation (.e. per column y). y numeric matrix response values. Rows correspond voxels/vertices/structures; columns observations. Row y response vector -th model fit. ... Named numeric matrices per-row (e.g. per-voxel) covariates. must dimensions y, argument name must match term referenced formula. row , row matrix substituted corresponding column data model fit.

title: Flexible Linear Model with Voxel-Varying Covariates — flexLm
text: numeric matrix nrow(y) rows 2 * p columns, p number columns model matrix. first p columns regression coefficients (named model-matrix columns); remaining p columns corresponding t-statistics, named tstatistic:<term>.

title: Flexible Linear Model with Voxel-Varying Covariates — flexLm

text: mincApply allows one execute R function every voxel set files. mincApply runs current R process; see also pMincApply qMincApply parallel variants. Unless function applied takes single argument, take voxel values every voxel, wrapper function written.  mincApply function also supports passing, instead function, quoted expression containing free variable 'x', taking voxel values; illustrated examples.  (supported pMincApply.) output function also simple vector scalar. Note interpreted R can slow. Mindnumbingly slow. therefore pays write optimal functions , whenever available, use optimized equivalents. words give one example, use mincLm rather applying lm, lm must applied, try use lm.fit rather plain lm. code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ getRMINCTestData() gf <- read.csv("/tmp/rminctestdata/test_data_set.csv") ma <- mincApply(gf$jacobians_fixed_2,quote(mean(x))); mincWriteVolume(ma, "means.mnc") ### run the whole thing in parallel on the local machine testFunc <- function (x) { return(c(1,2))} pout <- pMincApply(gf$jacobians_fixed_2,                    testFunc,                    workers = 4,                    global = c('gf','testFunc')) mincWriteVolume(pout, "pmincOut.mnc")  ### pbs example 1 (hpf) getRMINCTestData() gf <- read.csv("/tmp/rminctestdata/test_data_set.csv") testFunc <- function (x) { return(c(1,2))} options(TMPDIR="/localhd/$PBS_ID") pout <- pMincApply(gf$jacobians_fixed_2,                    testFunc,                    workers = 4,                    method="pbs",                    global = c('gf','testFunc'))  ### pbs example 2 (scinet) getRMINCTestData() gf <- read.csv("/tmp/rminctestdata/test_data_set.csv") testFunc <- function (x) { return(c(1,2))} options(WORKDIR="$SCRATCH") options(MAX_NODES=8) options(TMP_DIR="/tmp") pout <- pMincApply(gf$jacobians_fixed_2,                    testFunc,                    modules=c("intel","openmpi","R/3.1.1"),                    workers = 4,                    method="pbs",                    global = c('gf','testFunc'))  #NOTE 1: On SCINET jobs are limited to 32*48 hours of cpu time #NOTE 2: On SCINET the Rmpi library must be compiled for use with R 3.1.1 } # }

title: mincified version of lmer from lme4 — mincLmer
text: mincLmer provides interface running linear mixed effects models every voxel. Unlike standard mincLm, however, testing hypotheses linear mixed effects models difficult, since denominator degrees freedom difficult determine. RMINC provides two alternatives: (1) estimating degrees freedom using mincLmerEstimateDF function, (2) comparing two separate models using mincLogLikRatio (turn can corrected using mincLogLikRatioParametricBootstrap). likely models - longitudinal models separate intercept separate intercept slope per subject - approximations likely correct. careful using approximations using complicated random effects structures.  encounter memory issues, due minc file caching. Consider trying environment variable MINC_FILE_CACHE_MB set small value like 1.

title: mincified version of lmer from lme4 — mincLmer
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ vs <- mincLmer(filenames ~ age + sex + (age|id), data=gf, mask="mask.mnc") mincWriteVolume(vs, "age-term.mnc", "tvalue-age") # run in parallel with multiple processors on the local machine vs <- mincLmer(filenames ~ age + sex + (age|id),                data=gf,                mask="mask.mnc",                parallel=c("snowfall", 4)) # run in parallel with multiple processors over the sge batch queueing system vs <- mincLmer(filenames ~ age + sex + (age|id),                data=gf,                mask="mask.mnc",                parallel=c("sge", 4)) # estimate degrees of freedom vs <- mincLmerEstimateDF(vs) # correct for multiple comparisons using the False Discovery Rate (qvals <- mincFDR(vs)) # generate another model with a more complex curve for the age term library(splines) vs2 <- mincLmer(filenames ~ ns(age,2) + sex + (age|id), data=gf, mask="mask.mnc") # see if that more complex age term was worth it modelCompare <- mincLogLikRatio(vs, vs2) mincFDR(modelCompare) # see if there was any bias in those p-value estimates (takes a few minutes) modelCompare <- mincLogLikRatioParametricBootstrap(modelCompare) mincFDR(modelCompare) } # }
